title: The Heist That Made the Mona Lisa Famous: Andrew Lloyd Webber's Next Musical
summary:
Imagine this: you're at the Louvre in Paris, ready to sketch the Mona Lisa. You walk up to its spot on the wall — and it's gone. Just an empty wall with four hooks. That's exactly what happened on August 21, 1911. And now, Andrew Lloyd Webber — the composer behind 'The Phantom of the Opera,' 'Cats,' and 'Jesus Christ Superstar' — is writing a musical about this incredible true story.

Lloyd Webber, who is known for his love of spectacle (think a chandelier crashing down, or cats dancing on a giant tire), revealed the project in a recent interview. He already has a lot going on: he's writing a musical called 'The Illusionist' and his company just opened 'Cats: The Jellicle Ball,' a reimagining of his classic as a queer ballroom competition. But he couldn't resist hinting at 'the other one' — the Mona Lisa musical. 'More than that I cannot really tell you,' he said, 'for the simple reason that I'm going away next week to write it.'

So, what's the story? The Mona Lisa wasn't always the superstar it is today. Before the theft, it was well-known among art scholars but wasn't even the most famous painting in its gallery. Then Vincenzo Peruggia, an Italian glazier who had helped install the painting's protective glass, decided to take matters into his own hands. He put on a worker's smock, removed the painting from its case, and walked out. The theft wasn't discovered for 28 hours.

Peruggia hid the 30-inch-tall portrait in a trunk in his Paris apartment. When police came to interview him — because he worked at the Louvre — he didn't arouse any suspicion. For two years, the painting was gone. Peruggia eventually took it to Italy and tried to demand a ransom. He was arrested in December 1913.

Here's the twist: Peruggia claimed he was a hero who wanted to return the painting to Italy, its 'home country.' He even said he fell in love with her smile. Historians still debate his motives. But one thing is certain: the theft made the Mona Lisa a global phenomenon. Thousands of people, including writer Franz Kafka, crowded into the Louvre to stare at the empty wall. When the painting was recovered, it became the icon we know today.

Lloyd Webber's musical will tell this story of obsession, crime, and the birth of a celebrity. Expect dramatic songs, elaborate sets, and maybe even a glazier with a heart of gold — or a heart of thievery.
why_it_matters: This story reveals how a single criminal act can transform a piece of art into a global icon, raising questions about fame, cultural heritage, and the power of narrative to shape history.

keywords.explanation: The famous portrait by Leonardo da Vinci that was stolen in 1911 and later became a global icon.
The composer who is writing a musical about the Mona Lisa heist.
A theatrical performance with songs, which Lloyd Webber is creating about the theft.
The Italian glazier who stole the Mona Lisa and hid it for two years.
When Peruggia was caught by police in December 1913 after trying to demand a ransom.
A widely recognized symbol; the Mona Lisa became an iconic artwork after the theft.

questions.question: What project did Andrew Lloyd Webber hint at in a recent interview?
How long after the theft was the Mona Lisa discovered missing?
What did Vincenzo Peruggia claim as his motive?
options: He wanted to return the painting to Italy
He fell in love with the painting

questions.question: Where did Peruggia hide the Mona Lisa?
What happened to the Mona Lisa after it was recovered?
Which famous writer came to see the empty wall at the Louvre?

background_read: The Louvre museum in Paris was originally a royal palace and was opened to the public in 1793; it now holds over 35,000 artworks.
Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa between 1503 and 1519, and it was acquired by King Francis I of France.
Before the 1911 theft, the Mona Lisa was admired by art scholars but was not the most famous painting in its own gallery.
Vincenzo Peruggia had helped install the glass case that protected the painting, which gave him the knowledge to steal it.
After recovery, the painting toured Italy and then returned to the Louvre, where it has been a major tourist attraction ever since.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Hook that puts the reader in the moment of the theft.
└─ specific: 'Imagine this... you're at the Louvre... it's gone.'
KEY EVENT: The theft by Vincenzo Peruggia.
├─ WHO: Vincenzo Peruggia, an Italian glazier.
└─ HOW: He put on a smock, removed the painting, and walked out unnoticed.
AFTERMATH: Peruggia hides the painting for two years and is arrested.
├─ WHERE: Hidden in a trunk in his Paris apartment.
├─ POLICE INTERVIEW: He didn't arouse suspicion.
CONTEXT: The Mona Lisa wasn't famous before the theft.
└─ TWIST: Peruggia claimed he was a hero returning it to Italy.
RESULT: The theft made the painting a global phenomenon.
└─ EVIDENCE: Franz Kafka came to stare at the empty wall.
PRESENT: Andrew Lloyd Webber writing a musical about the story.
└─ OPEN: 'More than that I cannot really tell you.'

perspectives.description: The theft and the upcoming musical bring fresh attention to art history and may inspire a new generation to visit museums and learn about the Renaissance.
Glorifying a theft in a musical could be seen as celebrating a crime, and the original damage to the museum's security was a serious failure.
This case is a perfect example of the 'Borges effect' – how a work's fame can skyrocket after a dramatic event, independent of its artistic quality.
Lloyd Webber's musical may explore Peruggia's nationalism and the ethics of stealing for a 'noble cause,' prompting audiences to debate whether the ends justify the means.
